en_body:
Because Master's robe is light as twilight
Afternoon crosses the small courtyard, sunlight lingering on the threshold
The wind returns, three or four leaves fall
Does the little one gather yellow leaves, or lingering sorrow?
Because Master's eyes are sad as Winter's grief
The slender moon softly sheds one tender moon-drop
Seated amid the Buddha-altar, the sutra words desolate
Life is dust, Master's eyes are sad!...
At evening mist returns upon the shoulder
Master softly chants, pouring the deep bell-sound
Those eyes gaze sadly, infinitely far
Beyond the veranda sweeping leaves, the little one wonders.
Because Master's voice is deep as lullaby wind
Teaching the precious Dharma, life in the wheel of suffering.
Because the little one's heart is weightless as dew
At the end of the holy season, the little one grows thin!
(Iowa, Winter 1978)

Saintly
Because Master's robe is light as twilight
Afternoon crosses the small courtyard, sunlight lingering on the threshold
The wind returns, three or four leaves fall
Does the little one gather yellow leaves, or lingering sorrow?
Because Master's eyes are sad as Winter's grief
The slender moon softly sheds one tender moon-drop
Seated amid the Buddha-altar, the sutra words desolate
Life is dust, Master's eyes are sad!...
At evening mist returns upon the shoulder
Master softly chants, pouring the deep bell-sound
Those eyes gaze sadly, infinitely far
Beyond the veranda sweeping leaves, the little one wonders.
Because Master's voice is deep as lullaby wind
Teaching the precious Dharma, life in the wheel of suffering.
Because the little one's heart is weightless as dew
At the end of the holy season, the little one grows thin!
(Iowa, Winter 1978)
note: “Master” addresses a Buddhist monk, while “trầm-luân” evokes sentient life submerged in samsara's suffering.
